summary: Converted raw hexadecimal .4byte-encoded ASCII data in asm/sysdolphin/baselib/leak.s into readable .asciz string literals with explicit .balign 4 directives. The change affected leak-checker diagnostic strings and reduced noisy data declarations without changing the apparent string contents or labels.
what_changed: In lbl_80407B58, replaced a long sequence of .4byte hexadecimal constants representing null-terminated ASCII strings with direct .asciz declarations.
Inserted .balign 4 after each translated string to preserve the original 4-byte alignment expected by the assembly data layout.

Translated leak checker messages including "now registering suspecting memory ...
", "WARNING!!
", "Too many memory blocks are registerd to memory checker.
", "Begin memory leak check ...
", and leak detection format strings.
In .sdata at lbl_804D6004, replaced .4byte values for "done.\n" plus extra zero data with a single .asciz "done.\n".
The PR touched one file, adding 26 lines and deleting 125 lines.
smart_moves: Used .asciz rather than hand-decoded .4byte constants, making embedded diagnostics searchable and understandable for future decompilation work.
Preserved alignment with .balign 4 after each string, which is important because the original representation packed strings into word-sized data.
Kept the original labels and section placement, limiting the change to data representation rather than symbol or control-flow behavior.
Retained original spelling and punctuation from the binary strings, including apparent typos such as "registerd", avoiding accidental content changes.
decomp_lessons: When raw .4byte data clearly encodes ASCII strings, translating it to .asciz improves maintainability while keeping binary intent visible.
For word-encoded strings in assembly data sections, add explicit .balign 4 between strings if later data or label offsets depend on 4-byte alignment.
Do not silently correct typos in recovered strings; matching and evidence fidelity matter more than grammar.
String cleanup PRs can materially improve later function decompilation by exposing printf/assert/log format strings and making xrefs easier to search.
naming_conventions: No new symbol names were introduced; existing autogenerated labels such as lbl_80407B58, lbl_804D6000, and lbl_804D6004 were retained.
The file path follows the existing asm/sysdolphin/baselib subsystem layout.
Recovered strings reveal baselib leak-checker terminology such as "memory checker", "leak unregister range", "nb_reg", "mark", and "destroyed header".
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Translate groups of big-endian hexadecimal .4byte ASCII constants into equivalent .asciz strings.
Use .balign 4 after .asciz strings to reproduce the padding/alignment that was implicit in the previous word-based encoding.
Keep raw string contents byte-faithful, including newline escapes, null termination via .asciz, format specifiers like %p, %d, and %08x, and original misspellings.
For small .sdata strings, .asciz can replace multiple .4byte constants when no evidence suggests a required extra trailing word beyond normal string termination and alignment.
review_feedback: No issue comments or review comments were present in the provided PR context.
The PR had one review but the excerpt contains no review body or actionable feedback.
searchable_terms: Too many memory blocks are registerd
Please increase a capacity of checker
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that decompile or rename functions using these leak checker format strings.
Search for other assembly files with long .4byte ASCII sequences that can be safely translated to .asciz with alignment preserved.
Check whether lbl_80407B58 and lbl_804D6004 later received named symbols tied to baselib leak checker globals or string tables.
Audit translated strings for alignment-sensitive references before converting nearby raw data blocks.
